Dutch East Indies [7–1](/wiki/Football_at_the_1934_Far_Eastern_Championship_Games#Results "Football at the 1934 Far Eastern Championship Games") [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team")  ([Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila"), [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"); 13 May 1934)[[4]](#cite_note-rsssf_matches-4)[[5]](#cite_note-World_Elo_Ratings-5)  as **Indonesia**  [India](/wiki/India_national_football_team "India national football team") [3–0](/wiki/Football_at_the_1951_Asian_Games#Quarter-finals "Football at the 1951 Asian Games") Indonesia  ([New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), [India](/wiki/India "India"); 5 March 1951)[[5]](#cite_note-World_Elo_Ratings-5) | |
([1987](/wiki/Football_at_the_1987_SEA_Games "Football at the 1987 SEA Games"), [1991)](/wiki/Football_at_the_1991_SEA_Games "Football at the 1991 SEA Games") |

([Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"): *Tim nasional sepak bola Indonesia*) represents [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") in international men's [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches since 1945. The men's national team is controlled by the [Football Association of Indonesia](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Indonesia "Football Association of Indonesia") (PSSI), the governing body for [football in Indonesia](/wiki/Football_in_Indonesia "Football in Indonesia"), which is a part of [AFC](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation"), under the jurisdiction of [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"). Most of Indonesia home matches are played at the [Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium").

The **Indonesia national football team** ([Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"): *Tim nasional sepak bola Indonesia*) represents [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") in international men's [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") matches since 1945. The men's national team is controlled by the [Football Association of Indonesia](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Indonesia "Football Association of Indonesia") (PSSI), the governing body for [football in Indonesia](/wiki/Football_in_Indonesia "Football in Indonesia"), which is a part of [AFC](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation"), under the jurisdiction of [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"). Most of Indonesia home matches are played at the [Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium").

The team is colloquially referred to as *Tim Garuda* ([Garuda](/wiki/Garuda_Pancasila "Garuda Pancasila") Team), *Timnas* (National Team) or *Merah Putih* (The Red and White) after the country's [national emblem](/wiki/National_emblem_of_Indonesia "National emblem of Indonesia") and their distinctive red-and-white jerseys based on the [country's flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Indonesia "Flag of Indonesia"). Their fan club is known as *La Grande Indonesia* and *Ultras Garuda*.[[6]](#cite_note-evaluate-6)

Indonesia was the first team from Asia to participate in the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"). The predecessor of the team, [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies_national_football_team_results "Dutch East Indies national football team results"), participated in the [1938](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup") edition, where they were eliminated in the first round.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-afc-8) The team's only appearance at the [Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Football at the Summer Olympics") was in [1956](/wiki/Association_football_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics "Association football at the 1956 Summer Olympics").[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) Indonesia qualified for the [AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup") on five occasions, and advanced to the knockout stage for the first time in the [2023](/wiki/2023_AFC_Asian_Cup "2023 AFC Asian Cup") tournament, exiting in the round of 16.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Indonesia achieved the bronze medal at the [1958 Asian Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_1958_Asian_Games "Football at the 1958 Asian Games") in Tokyo.[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) The team appeared in the [ASEAN Championship](/wiki/ASEAN_Championship "ASEAN Championship") final six times. They finished runners-up on all six occasions. Indonesia has long-standing football rivalries with neighbours [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia_national_football_team "Malaysia national football team"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand_national_football_team "Thailand national football team") and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_national_football_team "Vietnam national football team").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Indonesia national football team](/wiki/History_of_the_Indonesia_national_football_team "History of the Indonesia national football team")

### 1921–1938: Colonization era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1921–1938: Colonization era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Het_Nederlands-Indi%C3%AB_Team_1935.jpg/250px-Het_Nederlands-Indi%C3%AB_Team_1935.jpg)](/wiki/File:Het_Nederlands-Indi%C3%AB_Team_1935.jpg)

Dutch East Indies squad's pioneer, the predecessor of the Indonesia national team

Dutch East Indies, or Netherlands East Indies was the national team that represented the territory of present-day Indonesia during the [Dutch colonial era](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies"). The team was organized by the Dutch East Indies Football Union (NIVU), which became a member of [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") on 24 May 1924. The first recorded unofficial match of the Dutch East Indies was against [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore_national_football_team "Singapore national football team") on 28 March 1921 in [Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_Dutch_East_Indies "Batavia, Dutch East Indies"), which ended in a 1–0 win. This was followed by matches in August 1928 against [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") (2–1 win) and in 1930 against a team from [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), China (4–4 draw).[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9)

On 19 April 1930, [Football Association of Indonesia](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Indonesia "Football Association of Indonesia") (PSSI) was founded with [Soeratin Sosrosoegondo](/w/index.php?title=Soeratin_Sosrosoegondo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Soeratin Sosrosoegondo (page does not exist)") as the first leader, after most all non-national organizations in the country such as [VIJ Jakarta](/wiki/Persija_Jakarta "Persija Jakarta"), [BIVB Bandung](/wiki/Persib_Bandung "Persib Bandung"), [PSM Mataram](/wiki/PSIM_Yogyakarta "PSIM Yogyakarta"), [IVBM Magelang](/wiki/PPSM_Magelang "PPSM Magelang"), [VVB Solo](/wiki/Persis_Solo "Persis Solo"), [MVB Madiun](/wiki/PSM_Madiun "PSM Madiun"), and [SIVB Surabaya](/wiki/Persebaya_Surabaya "Persebaya Surabaya") gathered at a meeting in [Soerakarta](/wiki/Surakarta "Surakarta") and agreed to form the second national football organization in order to resist the [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") control of the colonies by gathering all the footballers to play under PSSI.[[11]](#cite_note-kontan2-11)

In 1934, a team from [Java](/wiki/Java "Java") represented the Dutch East Indies in the [Far Eastern Games](/wiki/1934_Far_Eastern_Games "1934 Far Eastern Games") in [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila"), Philippines. The team managed to beat [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team") 7–1 in the first match, but the last two matches ended in defeat, 0–2 to [China](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team") and 2–3 to the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines_national_football_team "Philippines national football team"), resulting in the team finishing second in the tournament.[[12]](#cite_note-12) These matches are considered by [World Football Elo Ratings](/wiki/World_Football_Elo_Ratings "World Football Elo Ratings") to be the first matches involving the Indonesia national team.[[13]](#cite_note-eloratingsold-13) On 9 June 1935, two national organizations, NIVU and PSSI, joined forces and worked together to contribute players to the national team. NIVU remained the official football association of the Dutch East Indies and was admitted to FIFA membership on 14 August 1936, after first becoming a member in 1924.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### 1938–1945: Asia's first FIFA World Cup participant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1938–1945: Asia's first FIFA World Cup participant")]

[![Dutch East Indies squad for the 1938 edition of the World Cup](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/102014765_hindiabelandabareng.jpg/250px-102014765_hindiabelandabareng.jpg)](/wiki/File:102014765_hindiabelandabareng.jpg)

[![Dutch East Indies squad for the 1938 edition of the World Cup](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Dutch_East_Indies_1938_World_Cup_Squad.jpg/250px-Dutch_East_Indies_1938_World_Cup_Squad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dutch_East_Indies_1938_World_Cup_Squad.jpg)

Dutch East Indies squad for the [1938](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup") edition of the [World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup")

Dutch East Indies made its [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") debut during the [1938](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup") edition in [France](/wiki/France "France"). The team advanced to the final round without playing any qualifying matches. [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team"), who were drawn in Group 12 of the [qualifying round](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "1938 FIFA World Cup qualification") with the Dutch East Indies, decided to withdraw from the competition. The Dutch East Indies were then ordered by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") to carry out a [play-off](/wiki/Playoff_format "Playoff format") match against one of the Group 11 participants, the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team"). The match was scheduled to be played on 29 May 1938, but never took place after the United States also decided to withdraw from the competition. This allowed the Dutch East Indies to advance to the final round as the Asia's first World Cup participant.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[8]](#cite_note-afc-8)

In the final round, the Dutch East Indies were coached by [Johan Mastenbroek](/wiki/Johan_Mastenbroek "Johan Mastenbroek") who also served as chairman of the Dutch East Indies Football Association (NIVU). The team consisted of 17 players and was captained by a local Indonesian, [Achmad Nawir](/wiki/Achmad_Nawir "Achmad Nawir"). In the competition which still used the knockout format, the Dutch East Indies were eliminated in the first round when they were defeated by [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_national_football_team "Hungary national football team") 0–6. This match took place on 5 June 1938 at the [Stade Municipal](/wiki/Stade_Auguste-Delaune "Stade Auguste-Delaune"), [Reims](/wiki/Reims "Reims"). At the end of the competition, Hungary who had previously eliminated the Dutch East Indies, became the runner-up of the competition after being defeated by [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") 2–4 in the [final](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup_final "1938 FIFA World Cup final").[[16]](#cite_note-worldcup1938-16) Before [Curaçao](/wiki/Cura%C3%A7ao_national_football_team "Curaçao national football team") in [2026](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup"), the Dutch East Indies had been the only team representing a colony, before becoming an independent nation, to have qualified for a FIFA World Cup.

### 1945–1984: Independence era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1945–1984: Independence era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Indonesia_vs_Soviet_Union_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics.png/250px-Indonesia_vs_Soviet_Union_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics.png)](/wiki/File:Indonesia_vs_Soviet_Union_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics.png)

Indonesia's match against the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_football_team "Soviet Union national football team") at the [1956 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Association_football_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics "Association football at the 1956 Summer Olympics") in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"). The match ended in a goalless draw

After the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), followed by the [Indonesian Revolution](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Revolution "Indonesian National Revolution") in 1947, the national team no longer used the name Dutch East Indies and replacing it with the name Indonesia. The previous national football organization, the Dutch East Indies Football Union (NIVU) was succeeded by the [Football Association of Indonesia](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Indonesia "Football Association of Indonesia") (PSSI). The team's first official match under the name Indonesia was on 5 March 1951 at the [Asian Games](/wiki/1951_Asian_Games "1951 Asian Games"), where they lost 0–3 by the host nation [India](/wiki/India_men%27s_national_football_team "India men's national football team") at the [National Stadium, New Delhi.](/wiki/Major_Dhyan_Chand_National_Stadium "Major Dhyan Chand National Stadium")[[17]](#cite_note-asiangames1951-17)

Indonesia qualified for the [1956 Olympics](/wiki/Association_football_at_the_1956_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_team_squads "Association football at the 1956 Summer Olympics – Men's team squads") in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"). The team advanced directly to the quarter-finals of the tournament after the withdrawal of their first-round opponents, [South Vietnam](/wiki/South_Vietnam_national_football_team "South Vietnam national football team"). In the quarter-finals, the team met the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_football_team "Soviet Union national football team") who had previously defeated the [United Team of Germany](/wiki/United_Team_of_Germany_Olympic_football_team "United Team of Germany Olympic football team") 2–1. The match was held on 29 November 1956, where Indonesia forced the Soviet Union to a goalless draw. This resulted the match being replayed two days later where the team lost 0–4.[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9)

In 1957, Indonesia dominated the [qualifying round](/wiki/1958_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC_and_CAF) "1958 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and CAF)") of the [1958 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1958_FIFA_World_Cup "1958 FIFA World Cup"). The team defeated [China](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team") in the first round, then subsequently refused to play its next opponents, [Israel](/wiki/Israel_national_football_team "Israel national football team"), for political reasons.[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) Indonesian player [Rusli Ramang](/wiki/Rusli_Ramang "Rusli Ramang") became the top scorer at the end of the competition with 4 goals.

Indonesia won the bronze medal at the [1958 Asian Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_1958_Asian_Games "Football at the 1958 Asian Games") where the team beat [India](/wiki/India_men%27s_national_football_team "India men's national football team") 4–1 in the third-place match. The team also drew 2–2 with [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany_national_football_team "East Germany national football team") in a friendly match.[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) Indonesia won [Merdeka Tournament](/wiki/Merdeka_Tournament "Merdeka Tournament") trophy on three occasions (1961, [1962](/wiki/1962_Merdeka_Tournament "1962 Merdeka Tournament") and 1969). The team were also champions of the [1968 King's Cup](/wiki/1968_King%27s_Cup "1968 King's Cup").[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) Indonesia returned to World Cup qualification rounds in [1974](/wiki/1974_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "1974 FIFA World Cup qualification") as the team was eliminated in the [first round](/wiki/1974_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC_and_OFC)#Zone_B "1974 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and OFC)"), with only one win from six matches, against [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_men%27s_national_football_team "New Zealand men's national football team"). During the [qualification](/wiki/1978_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC_and_OFC) "1978 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and OFC)") round of [1978 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1978_FIFA_World_Cup "1978 FIFA World Cup"), Indonesia won a single of four matches, against the host team, [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore_national_football_team "Singapore national football team"). Four years later, in 1982, Indonesia won two [FIFA World Cup qualifying](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC_and_OFC) "1982 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and OFC)") matches, over [Chinese Taipei](/wiki/Chinese_Taipei_national_football_team "Chinese Taipei national football team") and [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team").[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9)

### 1985–1995: Renaissance of Indonesia football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1985–1995: Renaissance of Indonesia football")]

The [1986 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC)#AFC_Zone_B_(East) "1986 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)") round saw Indonesia advance from the first round with four wins, one draw, and one loss, eventually finishing at the top of its group. [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team") emerged victorious over Indonesia in the second round.[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) The team reached the semi-final of the [1986 Asian Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_1986_Asian_Games "Football at the 1986 Asian Games") after beating the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_national_football_team "United Arab Emirates national football team") in the quarter-finals. Indonesia then lost to host South Korea in the semi-finals and lost to [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait_national_football_team "Kuwait national football team") in the bronze medal match.[[18]](#cite_note-KUW-18)

A milestone during this era was the gold medal victory at the [Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/Southeast_Asian_Games "Southeast Asian Games") in both [1987](/wiki/Football_at_the_1987_Southeast_Asian_Games "Football at the 1987 Southeast Asian Games") and [1991](/wiki/Football_at_the_1991_Southeast_Asian_Games "Football at the 1991 Southeast Asian Games"). In 1987, Indonesia beat [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia_national_football_team "Malaysia national football team") 1–0; while in 1991, it beat [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand_national_football_team "Thailand national football team") in a [penalty shoot-out](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)").[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) In the [1990 FIFA World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC)#First_round "1990 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)"), the Indonesian team lost in the first round, with only one win against [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_national_football_team "Hong Kong national football team"), three draws and two defeats.[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) The team also only managed a single victory against [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_national_football_team "Vietnam national football team") in the [1994 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC)#Group_C "1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)") round.[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9)

### 1995–2016: Continental appearances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1995–2016: Continental appearances")]

#### 1996 AFC Asian Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1996 AFC Asian Cup")]

Indonesia's first appearance in the [AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup") was against [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_national_football_team "United Arab Emirates national football team") in the [1996 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/1996_AFC_Asian_Cup "1996 AFC Asian Cup"). During the tournament, Indonesia only scored a single point from a 2–2 draw against [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait_national_football_team "Kuwait national football team") in the first round.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

#### 1998 Tiger Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1998 Tiger Cup")]

In the final group stage match of the [1998 Tiger Cup](/wiki/1998_AFF_Championship "1998 AFF Championship"), Indonesia faced [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand_national_football_team "Thailand national football team") while the both teams already through to the semi-finals, but were also aware that the winner would have to face hosts [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_national_football_team "Vietnam national football team"). Indonesia's [Mursyid Effendi](/wiki/Mursyid_Effendi "Mursyid Effendi") deliberately kicked the ball into Indonesia's own goal as Thailand's attacker ran towards the ball.[[20]](#cite_note-20) FIFA fined both teams $40,000 for "violating the spirit of the game" while Effendi was banned from international association football for a lifetime. Indonesia was rewarded with a match by the team they wanted to face; minnows [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore_national_football_team "Singapore national football team") (who they ironically lost 2–1 to) in the semi-finals.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

#### 2000 AFC Asian Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2000 AFC Asian Cup")]

The team's second appearance in the Asian Cup was in [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") in the [2000 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2000_AFC_Asian_Cup "2000 AFC Asian Cup"); again, the Indonesian team gained only one point from three games, and again, from a match against Kuwait that finished without a score from either side. Indonesia established a higher record in the [2004 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2004_AFC_Asian_Cup "2004 AFC Asian Cup"), beating [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar_national_football_team "Qatar national football team") 2–1 to record the team's first-ever victory in the history of the tournament. The win was not enough for it to qualify for the second round, having fallen 0–5 to host [China](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team") and 1–3 to [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain_national_football_team "Bahrain national football team").

#### 2004 AFC Asian Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2004 AFC Asian Cup")]

Indonesia then qualified for their third successful tournament in the [2004 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2004_AFC_Asian_Cup "2004 AFC Asian Cup") being grouped with China, Qatar, and [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain_national_football_team "Bahrain national football team") with them winning the only match against Qatar in a 2–1 victory but crashed out of the tournament with only three points.

Indonesian starting line-up against [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain_national_football_team "Bahrain national football team")  at the first match of the group [2007 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium%2C_Asia_Cup_2007.jpg/250px-Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium%2C_Asia_Cup_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium,_Asia_Cup_2007.jpg)

Indonesian fans at [Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium") during the [2007 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup") match between Indonesia and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team")

The [2007 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup") saw Indonesia co-hosting the tournament with [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") becoming the first time in the AFC Asian Cup history that four countries co-hosted the competition. In the opening match of the tournament, Indonesia faces Bahrain with goals coming from [Budi Sudarsono](/wiki/Budi_Sudarsono "Budi Sudarsono") and [Bambang Pamungkas](/wiki/Bambang_Pamungkas "Bambang Pamungkas") to secure a 2–1 win. However, in the following two matches, Indonesia suffered a 2–1 defeat to [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team") and narrowly losing to [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team") 1–0 which Indonesia failed to qualified to the knockout stage of the tournament.

#### AFF Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: AFF Championship")]

Indonesia reached the finals of [AFF Championship](/wiki/AFF_Championship "AFF Championship") on six occasions (2000, 2002, 2004, 2010, 2016, and 2020), albeit never managing to lift the trophy victoriously. The team's claim of regional titles came in the [Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/Southeast_Asian_Games "Southeast Asian Games") of 1987 and 1991.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

After the [Peter Withe](/wiki/Peter_Withe "Peter Withe") era, the inability to fulfill the ASEAN target has been cited as the reason for Indonesia's "revolving door" in terms of team managers. Over two years, Indonesia's manager changed from Kolev to local coach [Benny Dollo](/wiki/Benny_Dollo "Benny Dollo") who was in turn sacked in 2010. The head coach position was then held by [Alfred Riedl](/wiki/Alfred_Riedl "Alfred Riedl") who failed to lift any cups and in July 2011 was then replaced by [Wim Rijsbergen](/wiki/Wim_Rijsbergen "Wim Rijsbergen").[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### 2012–2016: Suspensions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2012–2016: Suspensions")]

In March 2012, PSSI received a warning for the divided state of Indonesian football, whereby two separate leagues existed: the rebel [Indonesia Super League](/wiki/Indonesia_Super_League "Indonesia Super League") (ISL), which isn't recognized by PSSI or [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"), and the [Indonesia Premier League](/wiki/Indonesia_Premier_League "Indonesia Premier League") (IPL). The National Sports Committee (KONI) encouraged PSSI to work collaboratively with Indonesian Football Savior Committee (KPSI) officials to rectify the situation but KONI chairman Tono Suratman stated in March 2012 that KONI would take over the beleaguered PSSI if matters are not improved.[[26]](#cite_note-26) FIFA did not state whether Indonesia would face suspension, but on 20 March 2012, FIFA made an announcement. In the lead-up to 20 March 2012, PSSI struggled to resolve the situation and looked to its annual congress for a final solution.[[27]](#cite_note-27) PSSI was given until 15 June 2012 to settle the issues at stake, notably the control of the breakaway league; failing this, the case was to be referred to the [FIFA Emergency Committee](/wiki/FIFA_Emergency_Committee "FIFA Emergency Committee") for suspension.[[28]](#cite_note-28) FIFA eventually set a new 1 December 2012 deadline. In the two weeks preceding the deadline, three out of four PSSI representatives withdrew from the joint committee, citing frustrations in dealing with KPSI representatives. However, FIFA stated that it would only issue a punishment to Indonesian football after the Indonesian national squad finished its involvement in the [2012 AFF Championship](/wiki/2012_AFF_Championship "2012 AFF Championship").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In 2013, the president of PSSI [Djohar Arifin Husin](/wiki/Djohar_Arifin_Husin "Djohar Arifin Husin") signed a [Memorandum of understanding](/wiki/Memorandum_of_understanding "Memorandum of understanding") (MoU) with [La Nyalla Matalitti](/wiki/La_Nyalla_Matalitti "La Nyalla Matalitti") (KPSI-PSSI) that was initiated by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") and the [AFC](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation") through the [Asian Football Confederation](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation")'s Task Force. Since then, the control of [Indonesia Super League](/wiki/Indonesia_Super_League "Indonesia Super League") was taken by the Joint committee to remain manageable by *PT Liga Indonesia* until the establishment of a new professional competition by the committee.[[30]](#cite_note-30) This means the Indonesian players from [ISL](/wiki/Indonesia_Super_League "Indonesia Super League") were able to play and join the national team. The PSSI called players from both football leagues, ISL and [IPL](/wiki/Indonesian_Premier_League "Indonesian Premier League") to fortify the national team for the Asian Cup qualifier of 2015. On 7 January 2013, PSSI announced a list of 51 players from both sides of football leagues regardless of whether players from the breakaway Indonesia Super League (ISL) would make an appearance, allegedly ISL clubs were reluctant to release players because they doubted Djohar's leadership.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Timnas-irfan-bachdim.jpg/250px-Timnas-irfan-bachdim.jpg)](/wiki/File:Timnas-irfan-bachdim.jpg)

[Irfan Bachdim](/wiki/Irfan_Bachdim "Irfan Bachdim") in action during a friendly match against [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia_national_football_team "Malaysia national football team") at the [Gelora Delta Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Delta_Stadium "Gelora Delta Stadium") on 14 September 2014. Indonesia won 2–0

On 18 March 2013, PSSI held a congress at [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur"), Malaysia. Both parties, PSSI and KPSI (breakaway group) solved their differences in four contentious points; such as; Reunification of two leagues; Revision of the PSSI Statutes; Reinstatement of the four expelled PSSI Executive Committee members [La Nyalla Mattalitti](/wiki/La_Nyalla_Mattalitti "La Nyalla Mattalitti"), Roberto Rouw, Erwin Dwi Budiawan, and Toni Apriliani; and agreement of all parties to the Memorandum of Understanding from 7 June 2012 on the list of delegates to the PSSI Congress based on the list of the Solo Congress of July 2011. The new PSSI called 58 players from both sides leagues (ISL and IPL) for the national squad. [Rahmad Darmawan](/wiki/Rahmad_Darmawan "Rahmad Darmawan") returned as the caretaker coach of the national team with [Jacksen F. Tiago](/wiki/Jacksen_F._Tiago "Jacksen F. Tiago") as the assistant coach. They trimmed the 58 players initially called for national training to 28. The list would then be trimmed again to just 23 players for the [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team") match. [Victor Igbonefo](/wiki/Victor_Igbonefo "Victor Igbonefo"), [Greg Nwokolo](/wiki/Greg_Nwokolo "Greg Nwokolo") and [Sergio van Dijk](/wiki/Sergio_van_Dijk "Sergio van Dijk") the three naturalised players were on the final list.[[32]](#cite_note-32) On 23 March 2013, Indonesia was defeated 1–2 by Saudi Arabia at home. [Boaz Solossa](/wiki/Boaz_Solossa "Boaz Solossa") gave Indonesia the first goal in their campaign for AFC Asian Cup qualification; the home team started with the goal in the sixth minute but the Saudi Side fought back with the equalizer from [Yahya Al-Shehri](/wiki/Yahya_Al-Shehri "Yahya Al-Shehri") in the 14th minute before [Yousef Al-Salem](/wiki/Yousef_Al-Salem "Yousef Al-Salem") the scored what turned out to be the winner on 56th minute.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 2015, Football Association of Indonesia (PSSI) was suspended by FIFA due to government interference in the domestic competition. The announcement was made on 30 May 2015 and had an immediate impact on the national team. Indonesia would not be eligible to compete in the next round of qualifiers for the 2018 World Cup and 2019 Asian Cup, starting less than two weeks later. FIFA took action against Indonesia following a row between the local government and the football association which has resulted in the cancellation of the domestic competition.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The suspension was lifted at the [66th FIFA Congress](/wiki/FIFA_Congress "FIFA Congress").[[35]](#cite_note-35) By then, hurried perpetration was done for Indonesia in order to get in touch for the upcoming [2016 AFF Championship](/wiki/2016_AFF_Championship "2016 AFF Championship") where Indonesia eventually reached the final and once again fell to Thailand in process.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### 2017–2019: Rebuilding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2017–2019: Rebuilding")]

Some weeks after finishing as runners-up in the [2016 AFF Championship](/wiki/2016_AFF_Championship "2016 AFF Championship"), PSSI held a congress on 8 January 2017 in an effort to sign Spanish coach, [Luis Milla](/wiki/Luis_Milla_(footballer,_born_1966) "Luis Milla (footballer, born 1966)") to handle their senior and [U-22 team](/wiki/Indonesia_national_under-23_football_team "Indonesia national under-23 football team"). Prior to the [2018 AFF Championship](/wiki/2018_AFF_Championship "2018 AFF Championship"), Milla departed without any explanation, causing anger among Indonesian supporters.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Indonesia crashed out from the group stage in the [2018 AFF Championship](/wiki/2018_AFF_Championship "2018 AFF Championship"), which led to the sacking of [Bima Sakti](/wiki/Bima_Sakti "Bima Sakti").[[38]](#cite_note-38) In order to prepare for the [2022 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)") campaign, Indonesia signed Scottish coach [Simon McMenemy](/wiki/Simon_McMenemy "Simon McMenemy") with hope that his successful tenure with the Philippines could reinvigorate Indonesia's performance especially when Indonesia was grouped with three Southeast Asian rivals Malaysia, Thailand and Vietnam alongside [UAE](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_national_football_team "United Arab Emirates national football team").[[39]](#cite_note-39) Indonesia lost all four matches including a 2–3 home defeat to Malaysia despite having taken a 2–1 lead prior followed by a home loss to Vietnam for the first time in any competitive tournaments.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) On 6 November 2019, PSSI decided to sack McMenemy over the national team's deteriorating performance.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Indonesia traveled to Malaysia and lost 0–2 to its rival and was officially eliminated from the 2022 FIFA World Cup qualification.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### 2020–2025: Shin Tae-Yong era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 2020–2025: Shin Tae-Yong era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Shin_Tae-yong_Indonesia.jpg/330px-Shin_Tae-yong_Indonesia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shin_Tae-yong_Indonesia.jpg)

[Shin Tae-yong](/wiki/Shin_Tae-yong "Shin Tae-yong") guided Indonesia to the [2020 AFF Championship](/wiki/2020_AFF_Championship "2020 AFF Championship") final and the [2023 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2023_AFC_Asian_Cup "2023 AFC Asian Cup")

After failing to qualify for the World Cup, PSSI appointed [Shin Tae-yong](/wiki/Shin_Tae-yong "Shin Tae-yong") as the head coach of the Indonesian national team, becoming the first South Korean in Indonesia's managerial history. This appointment was based on the success of fellow Korean coach [Park Hang-seo](/wiki/Park_Hang-seo "Park Hang-seo") in Vietnam. Shin was then tasked with immediately forming a team to compete in the [2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification](/wiki/2023_AFC_Asian_Cup_qualification "2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification") round.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Under the management of Shin Tae-yong, the majority of the senior team was reshuffled to include many young players of whom the majority were from the [Indonesia national under-23 football team](/wiki/Indonesia_national_under-23_football_team "Indonesia national under-23 football team"). Indonesia made it to the [2020 AFF Championship](/wiki/2020_AFF_Championship "2020 AFF Championship") final with an average player age of 23.

#### 2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification")]

In the [2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification](/wiki/2023_AFC_Asian_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_third_round "2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification – third round"), Indonesia defeated host and former Asian champions, [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait_national_football_team "Kuwait national football team"), whom they had not defeated in 42 years, 2–1, to the surprise of many people, the first official win by a Southeast Asian team against a West Asian host since 2004 (when [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand_national_football_team "Thailand national football team") beat [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen_national_football_team "Yemen national football team") 3–0 in [Sanaa](/wiki/Sanaa "Sanaa") during the [2006 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)")), and was the first time in the history that a Southeast Asian team had won against a Persian Gulf team as the visitor. In the final match, Indonesia massively beat [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal_national_football_team "Nepal national football team") 7–0 at the [Jaber Al-Ahmad International Stadium](/wiki/Jaber_Al-Ahmad_International_Stadium "Jaber Al-Ahmad International Stadium"). Boosted by the win, Indonesia qualified for the upcoming [2023 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2023_AFC_Asian_Cup "2023 AFC Asian Cup") after a 16-year absence. Indonesia would be drawn with the top AFC rank team, [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team"), [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_national_football_team "Iraq national football team"), and Southeast Asia rival, Vietnam.

#### 2026 FIFA World Cup qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 2026 FIFA World Cup qualification")]

Indonesia began their 2026 FIFA World Cup qualification campaign in the first round, where they defeated [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei_national_football_team "Brunei national football team") 12–0 on aggregate to advance.

In the second round, Indonesia was drawn into a group with Iraq, Vietnam, and the Philippines, the same two teams they would later face in the 2023 AFC Asian Cup group stage. They opened the round with a 5–1 defeat to Iraq in [Basra](/wiki/Basra "Basra"), followed by a 1–1 draw against the Philippines in [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila").

#### 2023 AFC Asian Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 2023 AFC Asian Cup")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Soccer_Field_Transparant.svg/250px-Soccer_Field_Transparant.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Soccer_Field_Transparant.svg)

[**Ernando**](/wiki/Ernando_Ari "Ernando Ari")

[**Amat**](/wiki/Jordi_Amat_(footballer) "Jordi Amat (footballer)")

[**Walsh**](/wiki/Sandy_Walsh "Sandy Walsh")

[**Baggott**](/wiki/Elkan_Baggott "Elkan Baggott")

[**Asnawi**](/wiki/Asnawi_Mangkualam "Asnawi Mangkualam") ([c](/wiki/Captain_(association_football) "Captain (association football)"))

[**Pattynama**](/wiki/Shayne_Pattynama "Shayne Pattynama")

[**Jenner**](/wiki/Ivar_Jenner "Ivar Jenner")

[**Hubner**](/wiki/Justin_Hubner "Justin Hubner")

[**Yakob**](/wiki/Yakob_Sayuri "Yakob Sayuri")

[**Marselino**](/wiki/Marselino_Ferdinan "Marselino Ferdinan")

[**Struick**](/wiki/Rafael_Struick "Rafael Struick")

Indonesian starting line-up against [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") at the Round of 16 [2023 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2023_AFC_Asian_Cup "2023 AFC Asian Cup").

Indonesia then started off 2024 playing two friendly matches against [Libya](/wiki/Libya_national_football_team "Libya national football team") at the [Mardan Sports Complex](/wiki/Mardan_Sports_Complex "Mardan Sports Complex") in [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") before flying off to Qatar for the last friendly match against [Iran](/wiki/Iran_national_football_team "Iran national football team") as the final preparation for the 2023 AFC Asian Cup tournament, which Indonesia lost all the three matches.

In the first match, Indonesia faced off against Iraq just after two months of facing each other, in a 1–3 loss. In the second match, Indonesia faced off against Southeast Asia rival [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_national_football_team "Vietnam national football team") where captain, [Asnawi Mangkualam](/wiki/Asnawi_Mangkualam "Asnawi Mangkualam") converted a goal from the penalty spot to score the only goal in the match, it was the first time Indonesia defeated Vietnam after 7 years, as Indonesia gained 3 points. In the last group match, Indonesia lost 3–1 to the AFC top rank team, Japan.

Despite with two losses in the group stage, Indonesia qualified into Round of 16 by finishing as one of the four best third-place groups which was confirmed after another match in [Group F](/wiki/2023_AFC_Asian_Cup_Group_F "2023 AFC Asian Cup Group F") between [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan_national_football_team "Kyrgyzstan national football team") and [Oman](/wiki/Oman_national_football_team "Oman national football team") ended in a draw.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-asiancup2023bestthirdplace-47) For the first time ever, Indonesia passed through to the knockout stage of the AFC Asian Cup since their first appearance in [1996 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/1996_AFC_Asian_Cup "1996 AFC Asian Cup").[[47]](#cite_note-asiancup2023bestthirdplace-47) Indonesia faced against [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") in the Round of 16, and the team's run ended with a 4–0 loss.

#### Advanced to the third round of the 2026 FIFA World Cup qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Advanced to the third round of the 2026 FIFA World Cup qualification")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Vietnam_v_Indonesia_WCQ_2024_%28Indonesia_XI%29.jpg/250px-Vietnam_v_Indonesia_WCQ_2024_%28Indonesia_XI%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vietnam_v_Indonesia_WCQ_2024_(Indonesia_XI).jpg)

Indonesia line-up against [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_national_football_team "Vietnam national football team") in the [second round](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC)#Second_round "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)") of [2026 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)"), 26 March 2024. Indonesia won 3–0.

At the continuation of the [2026 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)"), Indonesia managed to beat Vietnam again twice on 21 & 26 March, 1–0 in [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") and 3–0 in [Hanoi](/wiki/Hanoi "Hanoi"); the latter would be the first time since 2004 that Indonesia defeated Vietnam in their own home ground. Thus Indonesia climbed up to the second place of the group with 7 points. The two victories also made Indonesia surpass ASEAN rivals, Philippines and Malaysia in the FIFA ranking, the latter would be the first time Indonesia placed above Malaysia in the FIFA ranking in 5 years.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

On 25 April 2024, PSSI president [Erick Thohir](/wiki/Erick_Thohir "Erick Thohir") announced that Shin's contract is officially extended until 2027.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Indonesia_v_Philippines_WCQ_2024_%28Haye%2C_Ridho_%26_Tjoe-A-On%29.png/250px-Indonesia_v_Philippines_WCQ_2024_%28Haye%2C_Ridho_%26_Tjoe-A-On%29.png)](/wiki/File:Indonesia_v_Philippines_WCQ_2024_(Haye,_Ridho_%26_Tjoe-A-On).png)

Indonesia's players celebrate a goal against the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines_national_football_team "Philippines national football team"), 11 June 2024. Indonesia won 2–0.

In June 2024, Indonesia concluded the second round of the FIFA World Cup qualifiers with two home matches. They suffered a 0–2 defeat to Iraq but secured a 2–0 victory over the Philippines in their final group match. With this result, Indonesia finished as Group F runner-up, earning qualification for the [2027 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2027_AFC_Asian_Cup "2027 AFC Asian Cup") and advancing to the [third round of World Cup qualification](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_AFC_third_round "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round") for the first time in history.[[51]](#cite_note-51) In the third round, Indonesia were drawn into Group C which consisted of powerhouse like [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team"), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team"), [China](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team"), and [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain_national_football_team "Bahrain national football team"). Indonesia started off their 2026 FIFA World Cup qualifiers in September 2024 where they draw 1–1 against Saudi Arabia in [Jeddah](/wiki/Jeddah "Jeddah") and a goalless draw with Australia at home.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) In October 2024, Indonesia draw 2–2 with Bahrain in [Riffa](/wiki/Riffa "Riffa") but however suffered a 2–1 defeat to China in [Qingdao](/wiki/Qingdao "Qingdao").[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) In November 2024, Indonesia suffered a heavy 4–0 defeat to Japan at home.[[56]](#cite_note-56) However, in the next match, Indonesia surprised the entire world with a shock as they defeated Saudi Arabia 2–0 with [Marselino Ferdinan](/wiki/Marselino_Ferdinan "Marselino Ferdinan") scoring a brace, thus breaking the record of Southeast Asian countries that have never beaten Saudi Arabia.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

#### 2024 ASEAN Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2024 ASEAN Championship")]

In the [2024 ASEAN Championship](/wiki/2024_ASEAN_Championship "2024 ASEAN Championship"), Indonesia fielded a squad primarily composed of players from the U-16 and U-20 teams, along with a few senior players who had participated in the 2023 AFC Asian Cup and the ongoing 2026 FIFA World Cup Qualification. The squad had an average age of 20.4 years.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Indonesia was placed in Group B, of which contained [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_national_football_team "Vietnam national football team"), [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines_national_football_team "Philippines national football team"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar_national_football_team "Myanmar national football team"), and [Laos](/wiki/Laos_national_football_team "Laos national football team").[[59]](#cite_note-59) Indonesia secured one victory but did not advance past the group stage.

#### Shin's Contract Termination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Shin's Contract Termination")]

On 6 January 2025, PSSI announced the termination of Shin Tae-yong's contract as Head Coach of the Indonesian Senior and U-23 National Teams.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The Indonesian FA President Erick Thohir said the dismissal was due to communication and tactical issues, with the defeat against China and failure in the ASEAN Championship being the determining factors.

The dismissal triggered significant backlash from supporters, who believed that Shin Tae-yong had played a major role in elevating the reputation of Indonesian football.

### 2025: Patrick Kluivert tenure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 2025: Patrick Kluivert tenure")]

Following Shin Tae-yong's dismissal, on 8 January 2025, the PSSI officially appointed [Patrick Kluivert](/wiki/Patrick_Kluivert "Patrick Kluivert") as head coach, with [Alex Pastoor](/wiki/Alex_Pastoor "Alex Pastoor") and [Denny Landzaat](/wiki/Denny_Landzaat "Denny Landzaat") assisting him with a contract until 2027.[[61]](#cite_note-coach2025-61) In the continuation of third round World Cup qualifiers, Indonesia's first match under Kluivert ended in a 1–5 away loss to Australia, but they bounced back by defeating Bahrain 1–0 at home. On 5 June 2025, Indonesia managed to defeat China 1–0, their first win against the latter in 38 years. The win advanced Indonesia to the [fourth round of World Cup qualification](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_AFC_fourth_round "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC fourth round").[[62]](#cite_note-62)

#### Elimination from the World Cup qualification and dismissal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Elimination from the World Cup qualification and dismissal")]

However, in the fourth round, Indonesia was beaten by [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team") 2–3 and [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_national_football_team "Iraq national football team") 0–1, thus eliminating Indonesia from the World Cup contention. Following the elimination, widespread fan disappointment sparked calls for Kluivert’s immediate dismissal. The anger intensified after Kluivert and his assistants returned to the Netherlands without issuing an apology or accepting responsibility for the defeat. On 16 October 2025, PSSI decided to parted ways with Kluivert and the rest of the coaching staffs through a mutual agreement.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### 2026: John Herdman appointment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: 2026: John Herdman appointment")]

On 3 January 2026, PSSI officially appointed former [Canada national team](/wiki/Canada_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Canada men's national soccer team") head coach [John Herdman](/wiki/John_Herdman "John Herdman") as the new head coach of the national team. Herdman will make his debut in the [2026 FIFA Series](/wiki/2026_FIFA_Series "2026 FIFA Series").[[64]](#cite_note-64)

## Team image

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Team image")]

### Kits and crest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Kits and crest")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4a/Commons-logo.svg/40px-Commons-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Commons-logo.svg)

Wikimedia Commons has media related to [Indonesia national football team kits](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Indonesia_national_football_team_kits "commons:Indonesia national football team kits").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Idn_kit.jpg/250px-Idn_kit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Idn_kit.jpg)

Indonesia's football jersey with number 17 in 1981

During the [Dutch colonial era](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies"), the team competed as Dutch East Indies in international matches and played in an orange jersey, the national color of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"). There are no official documents about the team's kit, only several black-and-white photos from the match against [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") in the [1938 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup") but unofficial documents stated that the kit consisted of an orange jersey, white shorts and light blue socks.[[65]](#cite_note-65)
Since [Indonesia's independence](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Indonesian_Independence "Proclamation of Indonesian Independence"), the kit consists of red and white, the colors of the [country's flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Indonesia "Flag of Indonesia"). A combination of green and white has also been used for the away kits and was used for the team's participation in the [1956 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics") in Melbourne, Australia, until the mid-1980s.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Erspo is the national team's [kit](/wiki/Kit_(association_football) "Kit (association football)") provider, a sponsorship that began in 2024 after the [AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2023_AFC_Asian_Cup "2023 AFC Asian Cup") in [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar"), and is contracted to continue until at least 2026.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Previously the team's kit was supplied by [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") and [Mills](/wiki/Mills_(sports_brand) "Mills (sports brand)"). Indonesia also wears other apparel, only when they compete in international sports events such as the [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games") and the [Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/Southeast_Asian_Games "Southeast Asian Games"). In those events Indonesia wears [Li-Ning](/wiki/Li-Ning "Li-Ning"), this is due to the Asian Games and SEA Games being multi-sports events all of whose contingents are under the Indonesian National Olympic Committee (NOC).[[68]](#cite_note-68)

From 2007 to 2019, [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") was the national team kit provider.[[69]](#cite_note-69) The 2010–2012 home kit became an issue when the team played against an opponent wearing an all-white uniform since the socks were white instead of the usual red. The solution was solved with a red-green-green combination (for away games) with green shorts and socks taken from the away kit, or initially an all-red uniform (for home games). After a home defeat in the [2014 World Cup third round qualifier](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_AFC_third_round "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round") match against [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain_national_football_team "Bahrain national football team") on 6 September 2011, the red shorts (with green application) were scrapped after its first outing and has never been used again. The red socks had white application on them, different from the red socks with green application worn during training. The combination of red-white-red was used sometimes in the future as the alternate home kit, for example on the subsequent home matches of the qualifiers against [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar_national_football_team "Qatar national football team") and [Iran](/wiki/Iran_national_football_team "Iran national football team") later that year.

On 31 May 2018, Nike released Indonesia's new home and away kits. The home shirt was red with a golden Nike logo inspired by the country's national emblem, the [Garuda Pancasila](/wiki/National_emblem_of_Indonesia "National emblem of Indonesia"). The home kit consisted of a red-white-red combination. The away kit consisted of a white-green-white combination with a green Nike logo on the shirt.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

In 2020, Indonesia started using new apparel from local brands [Mills](/wiki/Mills_(sports_brand) "Mills (sports brand)") until 2024. The home kit consists of a red-white-red combination with a silhouette in the front of the kit. The away kit consists white-green-white combination with a green horizontal strip across the front of the kit and a smaller white horizontal strip across the green strip. The third kit consists all black combination with golden strips and a silhouette in the front of the kit.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Further information: [Indonesia–Malaysia football rivalry](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_football_rivalry "Indonesia–Malaysia football rivalry") and [Indonesia–Vietnam football rivalry](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Vietnam_football_rivalry "Indonesia–Vietnam football rivalry")

Indonesia's main rival is [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia_national_football_team "Malaysia national football team"). It is one of Southeast Asia and Asia's best known [football rivalries](/wiki/Football_rivalries "Football rivalries").[[76]](#cite_note-guardian2-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77) The countries have played against each other 99 times, of which Indonesia have won 40, drawn 21 and lost 38. The heated political conflict between the two countries in the 1960s transmitted disease to the football field. The infamous "[*Ganyang Malaysia*!](/wiki/Malaysia-Indonesia_confrontation "Malaysia-Indonesia confrontation")" statement put forward by Indonesia's first president [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") in a 1963 political speech in Jakarta was considered an encouragement for the Indonesian team before matches against Malaysia.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

The first competitive match between the countries was a 4–2 win for Indonesia in the second round of the [1957 Merdeka Tournament](/wiki/1957_Merdeka_Tournament "1957 Merdeka Tournament") in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur"). Since then, the matches between the two, whether in [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") or Kuala Lumpur, have always been packed with spectators and have often resulted controversial incidents. In 2011, two fans died in a stampede during a [Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/Southeast_Asian_Games "Southeast Asian Games") final between the under-23 teams of Indonesia and Malaysia in Jakarta.[[76]](#cite_note-guardian2-76) The two teams most recently met in the [2020 AFF Championship](/wiki/2020_AFF_Championship "2020 AFF Championship"), with Indonesia winning 4–1.

Another of Indonesia's rivalries is based on its geographical proximity to neighboring countries in Southeast Asia such as [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand_national_football_team "Thailand national football team"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_national_football_team "Vietnam national football team"), and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore_national_football_team "Singapore national football team").

### Media coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Media coverage")]

The national team is always broadcast on [MNC Media](/wiki/MNC_Media "MNC Media"). But from 2020, MNC only covered the national team matches at [ASEAN Championship](/wiki/ASEAN_Championship "ASEAN Championship") and [AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup") due to MNC–[Lagardère](/wiki/Lagard%C3%A8re_Sports_and_Entertainment "Lagardère Sports and Entertainment") (ASEAN Championship) and AFC broadcasting rights partnership contract.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) However, other networks like [Emtek](/wiki/Emtek "Emtek") bought the rights from [PSSI](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Indonesia "Football Association of Indonesia") only.

Indonesia team qualifiers for the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)") and [2027 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2027_AFC_Asian_Cup_qualification "2027 AFC Asian Cup qualification") are broadcast by MNC Media's free-to-air television network [RCTI](/wiki/RCTI "RCTI") (1st round, 2nd round until matchday 5, 3rd round, & 4th round) and Emtek's free-to-air television network [Indosiar](/wiki/Indosiar "Indosiar") and [SCTV](/wiki/SCTV_(TV_network) "SCTV (TV network)") (matchday 6 of 2nd round only).[[81]](#cite_note-81)

## Home stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia_national_football_team&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Home stadium")]

Main articles: [Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium") and [Jakarta International Stadium](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Stadium "Jakarta International Stadium")

[![Gelora Bung Karno Stadium has been the home of the Indonesia national team since 1962](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Jakartacityofindonesia_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jakartacityofindonesia_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jakartacityofindonesia_(cropped).jpg)

[Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium") has been the home of the Indonesia national team since 1962

[![The Jakarta International Stadium during the FIFA U-17 World Cup group stage match between South Korea and the United States](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Jakarta_International_Stadium_interior.jpg/250px-Jakarta_International_Stadium_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jakarta_International_Stadium_interior.jpg)

The [Jakarta International Stadium](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Stadium "Jakarta International Stadium") during the [FIFA U-17 World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_U-17_World_Cup "FIFA U-17 World Cup") group stage match between South Korea and the United States

During Indonesia's early days as the Dutch East Indies, there was no stadium that could be recognized as a home ground since the national team never played home games in the country. The national team at that time played four games at neutral venues and one away game.[[9]](#cite_note-RSSSF-9) It was only after independence in 1945, the national team used the [Ikada Stadium](/wiki/Ikada_Stadium "Ikada Stadium") in [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") as their home ground.[[13]](#cite_note-eloratingsold-13) The stadium had a capacity of 30,000 spectators and was also used for the [National Sports Week](/wiki/National_Sports_Week "National Sports Week") in [1951](https://id.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pekan_Olahraga_Nasional_1951 "id:Pekan Olahraga Nasional 1951"). It was the largest stadium in Jakarta until 1962. The team used the stadium until 1963 after it was demolished to make way for construction of the [National Monument](/wiki/National_Monument_(Indonesia) "National Monument (Indonesia)"). The site is now known as [Merdeka Square](/wiki/Merdeka_Square,_Jakarta "Merdeka Square, Jakarta").[[82]](#cite_note-asiarooms1-82)

In 1962, [Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium") was inaugurated as Indonesia's new national stadium ahead of the [1962 Asian Games](/wiki/1962_Asian_Games "1962 Asian Games"). Located within the [Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Sports_Complex "Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex"), [Gelora](/wiki/Gelora,_Tanah_Abang "Gelora, Tanah Abang"), [Tanah Abang](/wiki/Tanah_Abang "Tanah Abang"), [Central Jakarta](/wiki/Central_Jakarta "Central Jakarta"), the stadium has a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of over 77,193 spectators and is currently the [40th largest association football stadium in the world](/wiki/List_of_association_football_stadiums_by_capacity "List of association football stadiums by capacity").[[6]](#cite_note-evaluate-6) Indonesia's first match at the stadium was played on 27 August 1962 against [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), where Indonesia won the match 6–0.[[8]](#cite_note-afc-8) Indonesia has used the stadium for almost every major home game, including the [2007 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup") matches.

In 2023, [Jakarta International Stadium](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Stadium "Jakarta International Stadium") was agreed to be the occasional home for the Indonesian national team, based on the agreement between the [Football Association of Indonesia](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Indonesia "Football Association of Indonesia") (PSSI) and PT JAKPRO as the developer.[[16]](#cite_note-worldcup1938-16) Located in [Tanjung Priok](/wiki/Tanjung_Priok "Tanjung Priok"), [North Jakarta](/wiki/North_Jakarta "North Jakarta"), the stadium was inaugurated in 2022 and has a capacity of 82,000 spectators, making it the largest stadium in Indonesia and largest [football-specific stadium](/wiki/Football-specific_stadium "Football-specific stadium") in Asia.[[17]](#cite_note-asiangames1951-17)[[18]](#cite_note-KUW-18) In addition, Indonesia also uses several stadiums to hold their home matches, such as [Gelora Bung Tomo Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Tomo_Stadium "Gelora Bung Tomo Stadium"), [Patriot Stadium](/wiki/Patriot_Stadium_(Indonesia) "Patriot Stadium (Indonesia)") and [Pakansari Stadium](/wiki/Pakansari_Stadium "Pakansari Stadium").

### Regional

* **[ASEAN Championship](/wiki/ASEAN_Championship "ASEAN Championship")**
  + ![2nd place, silver medalist(s)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Silver_medal_icon.svg/20px-Silver_medal_icon.svg.png) Runners-up (6): [2000](/wiki/2000_AFF_Championship "2000 AFF Championship"), [2002](/wiki/2002_AFF_Championship "2002 AFF Championship"), [2004](/wiki/2004_AFF_Championship "2004 AFF Championship"), [2010](/wiki/2010_AFF_Championship "2010 AFF Championship"), [2016](/wiki/2016_AFF_Championship "2016 AFF Championship"), [2020](/wiki/2020_AFF_Championship "2020 AFF Championship")

* **[Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_SEA_Games "Football at the SEA Games")**
  + ![1st place, gold medalist(s)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Gold_medal_icon.svg/20px-Gold_medal_icon.svg.png) **Gold medal (2):** [1987](/wiki/Football_at_the_1987_SEA_Games "Football at the 1987 SEA Games"), [1991](/wiki/Football_at_the_1991_SEA_Games "Football at the 1991 SEA Games")

  + ![3rd place, bronze medalist(s)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Bronze_medal_icon.svg/20px-Bronze_medal_icon.svg.png) Bronze medal (3): [1981](/wiki/Football_at_the_1981_SEA_Games "Football at the 1981 SEA Games"), [1989](/wiki/Football_at_the_1999_SEA_Games "Football at the 1999 SEA Games"), [1999](/wiki/Football_at_the_1999_SEA_Games "Football at the 1999 SEA Games")
